title: Cost Theory & Economies of Scale
simple_html:
Cost theory turns the production picture from Lesson 4 into money. It shows how a firm's costs change with output and why bigger is sometimes (but not always) cheaper per unit.

Fixed vs variable costs

Fixed costs (FC) do not change with output (rent, insurance). Variable costs (VC) rise with output (materials, hourly labor). Total cost TC = FC + VC.

Average and marginal cost

  • Average total cost (ATC) = TC / Q — cost per unit.
  • Average variable cost (AVC) = VC / Q.
  • Marginal cost (MC) = the cost of making one more unit.

In the short run these curves are U-shaped: per-unit costs fall at first (spreading fixed costs, gaining efficiency), then rise (diminishing returns). MC cuts ATC and AVC at their lowest points.

Economies of scale

In the long run, all costs are variable. Economies of scale mean long-run cost per unit falls as the firm grows (bulk buying, specialization, spreading R&D). Diseconomies of scale mean it rises when the firm gets too big to manage well. In between is the efficient size.

Key takeaways

  • TC = fixed + variable costs.
  • Short-run average cost curves are U-shaped.
  • MC intersects ATC and AVC at their minimums.
  • Economies of scale lower long-run unit cost; diseconomies raise it.
  • Ignore sunk fixed costs in short-run decisions (link to Lesson 1).
intermediate_html:
Cost theory translates the firm's technology into the cost curves that drive pricing and output decisions. Built on production theory (Lesson 4), it distinguishes short-run from long-run behavior and explains scale-related cost advantages.

Short-run cost concepts

With capital fixed, costs split into fixed costs (FC) — independent of output — and variable costs (VC) — rising with output. From TC = FC + VC we derive the per-unit family: average fixed cost AFC = FC/Q (always falling, "spreading the overhead"), average variable cost AVC = VC/Q, average total cost ATC = TC/Q = AFC + AVC, and marginal cost MC = ΔTC/ΔQ = ΔVC/ΔQ.

Why the curves are U-shaped

AVC, ATC, and MC are U-shaped because of the law of diminishing marginal returns from Lesson 4: marginal cost is inversely related to marginal product (MC = w/MPL). When MPL rises, MC falls; when MPL falls (diminishing returns), MC rises. Two key geometric facts: (1) MC intersects both AVC and ATC at their minimum points; (2) when MC is below an average curve it pulls the average down, and when above it pulls the average up. The vertical gap between ATC and AVC is AFC, which narrows as output grows.

The shutdown and break-even points

These curves locate two decision thresholds. The break-even point is at minimum ATC (price there yields zero economic profit). The shutdown point is at minimum AVC: if price falls below minimum AVC, the firm cannot even cover variable costs and should halt production in the short run (it would lose less by paying only its fixed costs). Fixed costs are sunk in the short run and irrelevant to this choice — the discipline from Lesson 1.

Worked example

FC = $1,000. At Q = 100, VC = $1,500. Then TC = $2,500; ATC = $25; AVC = $15; AFC = $10. Suppose producing the 101st unit raises VC by $18, so MC = $18. Since MC ($18) > ATC ($25)? No — MC $18 < ATC $25, so ATC is still falling. But MC $18 > AVC $15, so AVC is rising. If the market price were $14, it is below AVC ($15): the firm should shut down. At a price of $30 it covers ATC and earns profit.

Long-run costs and economies of scale

In the long run all inputs vary, so there are no fixed costs. The long-run average cost (LRAC) curve is the envelope of all possible short-run ATC curves — for each output, the firm picks the plant size with the lowest cost. The LRAC's shape reflects returns to scale:

RegionReturns to scaleLRAC
Economies of scaleIncreasingFalling
Constant returnsConstantFlat (minimum)
Diseconomies of scaleDecreasingRising

The lowest output at which LRAC is minimized is the minimum efficient scale (MES) — a key determinant of how many firms an industry can support (links to market structure, Lesson 6).

Sources of scale economies and scope

  • Economies of scale: bulk purchasing, specialization of labor, spreading fixed R&D/marketing, technical (engineering) relationships.
  • Diseconomies of scale: coordination and communication breakdowns, bureaucracy, weakened incentives.
  • Economies of scope: producing two products together more cheaply than separately (shared inputs), distinct from scale.

How to apply it

Use MC for pricing and output (it meets MR in Lesson 6); use AVC to decide whether to keep operating; size your plant to the MES; and pursue scale/scope economies as a cost-based competitive advantage.
full_html:
Cost theory monetizes the production technology of Lesson 4, producing the cost curves that are indispensable for the supply, pricing, and market-structure analysis that follows. It separates the short run (some inputs fixed) from the long run (all inputs variable), explains the characteristic shapes of cost curves from the underlying production relationships, and analyzes economies of scale and scope as foundations of competitive advantage.

Opportunity cost foundations

Economic cost is opportunity cost (Lesson 1): it includes implicit costs (the forgone return on owner-supplied resources) alongside explicit outlays. Hence economic cost exceeds accounting cost, and economic profit is correspondingly lower. Sunk costs — irrecoverable past expenditures — are excluded from forward-looking decisions. This distinction governs the shutdown decision below and is the reason fixed costs, once sunk, are irrelevant to short-run output choices.

Short-run costs and their derivation from production

In the short run, TC = FC + VC(Q). The per-unit measures are AFC = FC/Q, AVC = VC/Q, ATC = AFC + AVC, and MC = dTC/dQ. The shapes are not assumed but derived from production: with a single variable input at wage w, VC = w·L, so MC = w/MPL and AVC = w/APL. Therefore MC and AVC are mirror images of the marginal- and average-product curves of Lesson 4: as MPL rises MC falls, and once diminishing marginal returns set in (MPL falling) MC rises — generating the U-shape. AFC declines monotonically (FC spread over more units), so ATC is U-shaped and asymptotically approaches AVC. The marginal-average relationship is exact: MC passes through the minima of both AVC and ATC, lying below an average when it is falling and above when it is rising. Minimum AVC occurs at a lower output than minimum ATC because rising AFC delays ATC's turning point.

Break-even and shutdown decisions

Two thresholds emerge. The break-even point is min ATC: a competitive firm earns zero economic profit when P = min ATC. The shutdown point is min AVC: for P < min AVC the firm's revenue fails to cover even variable cost, so it minimizes loss by ceasing production and bearing only its (sunk) fixed cost. For min AVC ≤ P < min ATC the firm operates at a loss in the short run but continues because it covers all variable cost and some fixed cost. The short-run supply curve of a competitive firm is therefore the MC curve above min AVC. These results, due to Marshall's (1890) marginal apparatus, directly apply the relevant-cost logic of Lesson 1.

Long-run cost: the LRAC envelope

In the long run all inputs adjust, so the firm can choose plant size. The long-run average cost (LRAC) curve is the lower envelope of the family of short-run ATC curves: at each output the firm selects the plant minimizing cost (Viner, 1931 — whose draftsman's famous error about the envelope not passing through SRATC minima is a classic teaching point: LRAC is tangent to each SRATC, touching the SRATC minimum only at the LRAC's own minimum, where returns to scale are constant). The shape of LRAC encodes returns to scale from Lesson 4: increasing returns ⇒ falling LRAC (economies of scale), constant returns ⇒ flat LRAC, decreasing returns ⇒ rising LRAC (diseconomies of scale). Many empirical LRAC curves are L-shaped — falling then flat — implying a wide range of efficient sizes.

Minimum efficient scale and market structure

The minimum efficient scale (MES) is the smallest output at which LRAC reaches its minimum. MES relative to market demand is a primary determinant of industry concentration (Bain, 1956): when MES is large relative to the market, only a few firms fit (natural oligopoly or, in the extreme, natural monopoly with continuously falling LRAC), whereas a small MES supports many competitors. This is the structural bridge to Market Structures (Lesson 6).

Sources of economies, diseconomies, and scope

Economies of scale arise from labor specialization (Smith, 1776), indivisibilities and the spreading of fixed costs (R&D, advertising, ERP systems), engineering/volume relationships (the "two-thirds rule" for container costs), bulk-purchasing power, and learning. Diseconomies of scale stem from managerial coordination limits, communication and bureaucratic friction, and attenuated incentives in large hierarchies — Coase's (1937) explanation for why firms do not grow without bound. Distinct from scale, economies of scope (Panzar & Willig, 1981) exist when joint production of multiple outputs is cheaper than separate production, C(Q1,Q2) < C(Q1,0) + C(0,Q2), owing to shared inputs (a distribution network carrying many SKUs). The learning (experience) curve (Wright, 1936; BCG, 1970s) is a dynamic cost reduction with cumulative output, conceptually separate from static scale economies.

Worked example

A plant has FC = $2,000. Variable cost is VC = 5Q + 0.05Q². Then TC = 2,000 + 5Q + 0.05Q²; MC = dTC/dQ = 5 + 0.1Q; AVC = 5 + 0.05Q; ATC = 2,000/Q + 5 + 0.05Q. Min ATC: set d(ATC)/dQ = -2,000/Q² + 0.05 = 0 ⇒ Q² = 40,000 ⇒ Q = 200, where ATC = 10 + 5 + 10 = $25 and MC = 5 + 20 = $25 (confirming MC = ATC at the minimum). AVC is minimized as Q → 0 here (5), so the shutdown price is essentially $5. At a market price of $25 the firm breaks even; below $5 it shuts down. This shows how the algebra of cost curves locates the exact pricing thresholds used in Lesson 6.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Confusing economies of scale (long-run, all inputs, slope of LRAC) with diminishing returns (short-run, one input, shape of MC).
  • Using ATC instead of MC for output decisions; only marginal cost meets marginal revenue.
  • Including sunk fixed costs in the shutdown decision; only AVC matters in the short run.
  • Believing LRAC passes through the minimum of every SRATC (Viner's envelope error).
  • Conflating economies of scope with economies of scale, or scale economies with learning-curve effects.
  • Assuming bigger is always cheaper — diseconomies eventually dominate.

Connections across the syllabus

Cost theory completes the supply side begun in Production Theory (Lesson 4): MC = w/MPL and AVC = w/APL tie the curves to marginal and average product. The MC curve becomes the firm's supply schedule and meets marginal revenue in the MR = MC rule of Market Structures (Lesson 6) and Pricing Practices (Lesson 7). MES and natural-monopoly conditions shape industry concentration (Lesson 6) and motivate regulation in Market Failure (Lesson 9). The shutdown/break-even thresholds operationalize the relevant-cost and opportunity-cost principles of Lesson 1. Cost curves are thus the hinge between the firm's internal technology and its behavior in the marketplace.
